title: Why the West stopped making land - Works in Progress Magazine
gist: US and Western urban land reclamation stopped abruptly in the 1970s not for geographic or technological reasons but because of 1970s environmental laws (NEPA, Clean Water Act), even as downtown land values rose 30x and now exceed reclamation costs by 70x; reforming these laws could unlock hundreds of thousands of homes near city centers.

insights.claim: No major urban land reclamation project has happened in the United States since Battery Park City was completed in 1976, despite land values and flooding risk both rising sharply.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Frames a multi-decade structural land-supply constraint in the most valuable US cities — relevant to coastal urban land/housing scarcity theses.
insights.quote: Since the completion of Battery Park City in 1976, there has not been a single major urban land reclamation project in the United States and only a handful of port expansions.
insights.locator: Today, reclamation should be more common

insights.claim: The annual rate of US wetland loss in the 1960s was eight times higher than in the 1990s and twenty times higher than in the 2010s, marking an abrupt 1970s break rather than a gradual decline.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: The sharp, simultaneous break is the empirical case that regulation (not geography/tech) caused the stop — supports a policy-reversibility thesis.
insights.quote: The annual rate of wetland loss in the 1960s was eight times higher than in the 1990s, and twenty times higher than in the 2010s.
insights.locator: The abruptness of the change shows up

insights.claim: Of 37 US cities with data, every one reclaimed land more slowly after 1970, with cities like Seattle and Chicago slowing to zero.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Nationwide simultaneity rules out region-specific resource exhaustion, strengthening the regulatory-cause argument.
insights.locator: Of the 37 cities for which we can find data

insights.claim: An acre of single-family-zoned land in San Francisco County averages $24 million, more than 70 times the roughly $330,000-per-acre 2025-dollar cost estimated to reclaim Bay land.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A 70x value-to-cost gap implies enormous latent profit in coastal land creation if permitting were relaxed — a direct real-estate/policy arbitrage.
insights.quote: the cost of an acre of single-family-zoned land in San Francisco County averages $24 million, more than 70 times the estimated reclamation cost.
insights.locator: Today, downtown land values are high enough

insights.claim: Real housing prices are at least five times higher today than in 1970 in New York, San Francisco, Boston, and LA, and three times higher nationally, raising the value of foregone construction to record levels.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quantifies the opportunity cost of supply constraints — supports structural-housing-scarcity and pro-supply-reform positioning.
insights.quote: Real housing prices are at least five times higher today than in 1970 in New York, San Francisco, Boston, and LA
insights.locator: Simultaneously, the value of foregone construction

insights.claim: Hundreds of square miles of water near major US cities are shallow enough to reclaim easily, including two-thirds of San Francisco Bay (shallower than 20 feet), the under-six-foot South Bay near Silicon Valley, most of New York harbor under ten feet, and the waters between Miami and Miami Beach.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Refutes the 'ran out of good spots' objection; identifies specific high-value geographies where reform would unlock buildable land.
insights.quote: Two thirds of the San Francisco Bay is shallower than this. The South Bay, adjoining Silicon Valley, is less than six feet deep.
insights.locator: Hundreds of square miles of water

insights.claim: Dredging capacity has grown nearly tenfold since 1950 while land in major cities now sells for ten to a hundred times the $40-per-square-foot level that made Boston's Back Bay profitable.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Both supply-side capability and demand-side value have improved, meaning only legal barriers prevent profitable reclamation.
insights.quote: Land in major cities today sells for ten to a hundred times that, and dredging capacity has grown nearly tenfold since 1950.
insights.locator: Despite the space opened up by cars

insights.claim: Average US commute times have risen by a quarter since 1980 as transportation tech stagnated and highway construction now costs at least four times as much per mile as in 1960.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Killed the 'transport made reclamation unnecessary' substitute; rising infra costs reinforce the value of central, reclaimed land.
insights.quote: highway construction costs at least four times as much per mile as it did in 1960.
insights.locator: After rapid improvements in the twentieth century

insights.claim: Since 1990 the post-WWII flight from US city centers reversed and central land values have grown quickly despite continued suburban expansion, because cities concentrate jobs and generate knowledge spillovers.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Central-land premium is durable, not cyclical — supports long-run bullishness on dense urban core real estate.
insights.quote: But since 1990, this trend has reversed, and the value of land in city centers has grown quickly despite continuing suburban expansion.
insights.locator: Crime and pollution pushed people out

insights.claim: Environmental impact statements filed with the US Army Corps of Engineers (the permitting agency for reclamation) take an average of six years to complete, with mitigation often running into the hundreds of millions of dollars.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quantifies the regulatory tax on any waterfront/coastal infrastructure project — a key risk factor for timelines and IRR.
insights.quote: Environmental impact statements filed by the US Army Corps of Engineers ... take an average of six years to complete, and the mitigation measures often run into the hundreds of millions of dollars.
insights.locator: Land reclamation must always go through

insights.claim: Norfolk's Craney Island extension has seen almost nothing built 28 years after its 1997 feasibility study, with a nine-year/1,400-page EIS and a 2023 plan change forcing yet another review; it is now expected to finish in 2065 versus the original island's two-year build (1956-1958).
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Concrete illustration of multi-decade permitting drag — a template risk for any US port-expansion or dredging investment.
insights.quote: Twenty-eight years after the feasibility study began, almost nothing has been built. The project is expected to finish 'on schedule' in 2065.
insights.locator: The Craney Island extension in Norfolk

insights.claim: NEPA's open-ended detail requirements have, via decades of litigation, turned impact reports into thousand-page documents — New York's congestion pricing environmental assessment ran 900 pages plus 3,000 pages of appendices despite requiring zero major construction.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Litigation risk, not statute text, drives cost — implies reform must address judicial review, not just paperwork standards.
insights.quote: The environmental assessment for New York City's congestion pricing plan, for example, is 900 pages long with 3,000 pages of appendices, despite the project requiring zero major construction.
insights.locator: NEPA requires covered projects to prepare

themes.theme: Global divergence: who still makes land

insights.claim: China still leads the world in reclamation, accounting for close to 80 percent of all land reclaimed in the 21st century and adding land equivalent to 39 Manhattans within its million-plus cities since 2000, including a new city of 500,000 outside Shanghai.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Demonstrates reclamation remains technically feasible at scale; the West's stop is policy, not capability — relevant to dredging/construction demand geography.
insights.quote: China still leads the world in land reclamation, with close to 80 percent of all land reclaimed in the twenty-first century.
insights.locator: But China still leads the world

insights.claim: Japan sustains reclamation despite NEPA-style environmental review by using clear numeric thresholds for when reports are required and courts that make agency decisions hard to overturn, removing the litigation defense that makes Western projects take decades.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A concrete, transplantable reform model: the binding constraint is judicial reviewability and threshold ambiguity, not environmental standards per se.
insights.quote: Japan's system has two advantages: clear numeric thresholds for when reports are required and courts that make it harder to overturn agency decisions.
insights.locator: Japan does have high environmental standards

insights.claim: Western reclamation collapsed simultaneously across countries that passed environmental laws: Germany's Wadden Sea reclamation ended in the late 1980s, France banned construction within 100 meters of the shore in the 1980s, and the Dutch abandoned their Markermeer polder after 1969-era spatial-planning rules and the 1979 European Birds Directive.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Cross-country synchronicity is the strongest natural experiment that regulation is the cause — generalizes the thesis beyond the US.
insights.quote: What western nations shared was the rise of powerful environmental movements hostile to reshaping the landscape.

insights.claim: Even the rare modern Western success, Rotterdam's Maasvlakte 2 port expansion, took eighteen years including an eleven-year planning/environmental compliance period, with an environmental review of some 6,000 pages.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Even successful Western projects carry decade-plus compliance lead times — a structural drag on European port/infrastructure capex returns.
insights.quote: took eighteen years to complete, including an eleven-year planning and environmental compliance period before construction began. Its environmental review alone ran to some six thousand pages.
insights.locator: Even the rare successes are instructive

themes.theme: The reform thesis and second-order effects

insights.claim: Lifting legal barriers to reclamation could build hundreds of thousands of new homes near the centers of the most valuable US cities, new airports, and coastal flood defenses — framed as a reversible policy choice.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Core actionable thesis: a permitting-reform catalyst would unlock a large new supply of high-value urban land and housing.
insights.quote: If the legal barriers to reclamation were lifted, we could build hundreds of thousands of new homes near the centers of our most valuable cities.
insights.locator: If the legal barriers to reclamation

insights.claim: Reclamation is argued to be net environmentally positive in some cases because city dwellers emit far less carbon per person than suburbanites, so denser cities may outweigh lost aquatic habitat while also adding coastal disaster resilience.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Reframes reclamation as pro-climate rather than anti-environment — the political wedge that could enable reform.
insights.quote: the environmental benefits of bringing more people into dense, efficient cities where they have far lower emissions per capita may outweigh the loss of aquatic habitat all on its own

insights.claim: Depth is not a hard limit when land values are high: Monaco's Mareterra project expanded the country's land area by three percent by dredging down to 164 feet, and Singapore's Jurong Island was built over 50 feet of water atop 50 feet of soft marine clay.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Shows engineering feasibility scales with land value, removing the technical-limit objection for high-value US sites.
insights.quote: Monaco's recent Mareterra project expanded the country's land area by three percent by dredging down to 164 feet.
insights.locator: Nor is depth a hard limit
